options.label: C
options.text: Processes waiting for each other forever

explanation: A deadlock is a set of processes each blocked holding one resource and waiting on a resource held by another process in the set, so none of them can ever proceed. The key word is 'forever': the processes are alive but permanently blocked, not terminated (A) and not destroying each other (B). Remember the four Coffman conditions that must all hold: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait.

stem: Given a figure of 5 gates, if delay of each gate is 1T, what is total delay? (similar to the figure below)
explanation: Propagation delay is the length of the longest path from an input to the output (the critical path), not the total number of gates. The circuit has 5 gates arranged in 3 levels (inverters feeding two middle gates feeding one output gate), so a signal passes through 3 gates: 3 x 1T = 3T. Option C (5T) is the trap: it sums all 5 gates, which would only be right if they were all in series.

RISC is defined by a small set of simple, fixed-length instructions that mostly execute in one cycle, which is what makes deep pipelining and hardwired control possible. Options B and C describe CISC: a large instruction set with instructions that can operate directly on memory operands. In RISC only load and store touch memory (load/store architecture); everything else works on registers.

stem: Which of the following is not Multimedia?
explanation: Multimedia networking is conventionally split into exactly three classes: streaming stored audio/video, streaming live audio/video, and real-time interactive (voice/video over IP). 'Sensitive streaming' is not one of them, it is an invented term, so it is the odd one out. Do not confuse it with the property that multimedia traffic is delay-sensitive but loss-tolerant, which is probably where the distractor's wording came from.

Two's complement = invert every bit, then add 1. Invert 1111 1111 1111 -> 0000 0000 0000, then add 1 -> 0000 0000 0001. Sanity check with values: 1111 1111 1111 is -1 in 12-bit two's complement, and negating -1 gives +1, which is exactly 0000 0000 0001. The classic slip is stopping at the one's complement (all zeros) and forgetting the +1.

An ARP request is broadcast on the LAN, so the Ethernet destination MAC is FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF, which is all 1's, and that is the intended answer. The stem is loose: inside the ARP payload the target hardware address field is filled with all 0's precisely because it is the unknown being asked for. Rule of thumb: the frame's destination is all 1's (broadcast), the unknown field being queried is all 0's.

stem: What does a node do in a linked list?
answerText: Each element in a linked list is called a node. A single node contains data and a pointer to the next node which helps in maintaining the structure of the list. The first node is called the head; it points to the first node of the list and helps us access every other element in the list.
explanation: A node is the single element of a linked list: it stores a data field plus a pointer (link) to the next node, and in a doubly linked list also a pointer to the previous node. The chain of next-pointers starting from the head is what holds the list together, which is why a list has no random indexing and must be traversed from the head, giving O(n) access but O(1) insert/delete once you hold the node.

stem: Difference between Moore and Mealy FSM
answerText: Moore depends on present state, Mealy depends on present state and present input.
explanation: The difference is only in the output function: a Moore machine's output depends on the present state alone, while a Mealy machine's output depends on the present state AND the present input. Consequences worth quoting: Mealy usually needs fewer states and reacts in the same cycle as the input, but its outputs can glitch with the inputs; Moore's outputs are stable/registered but appear one clock later. The next-state logic is the same for both (state and input).

stem: What is the port of HTTP?
explanation: HTTP uses well-known TCP port 80. Pair it with HTTPS on 443, which is the distractor people reach for; other common ones on the same exam sheet are FTP 20/21, SSH 22, Telnet 23, SMTP 25, DNS 53 and DHCP 67/68.

answerText: Translation Look Aside Buffer: memory cache that is used to reduce the time taken to access a user memory location.
explanation: TLB = Translation Lookaside Buffer, a small fully/highly associative cache inside the MMU that holds recently used virtual-page-to-physical-frame translations. On a TLB hit the physical address is formed without touching memory; on a miss the hardware or OS walks the page table in memory (slow) and then loads the entry into the TLB. It caches address translations, not data, which is what separates it from the L1/L2 data cache.

answerText: A computer trapdoor, also known as a back door, provides a secret -- or at least undocumented -- method of gaining access to an application, operating system or online service. Programmers write trapdoors into programs for a variety of reasons.
explanation: A trap door (back door) is a hidden, undocumented entry point deliberately left in a program, OS or service that bypasses the normal authentication and access controls. Programmers add them for debugging or maintenance convenience, and attackers exploit or plant them for persistent access.

stem: Definition of propagation delay, rate & throughput
answerText:
Propagation delay: After the packet is transmitted to the transmission medium, it has to go through the medium to reach the destination. Hence the time taken by the last bit of the packet to reach the destination is called propagation delay.
Rate: The speed at which data is transferred within the computer or between a peripheral device and the computer, measured in bytes per second.
Throughput: s a measure of how many units of information a system can process in a given amount of time
explanation: Propagation delay is the time a bit spends travelling through the physical medium, distance / propagation speed, and it depends on link length, not on packet size. Rate (bandwidth) is the link's nominal transfer speed in bits per second, while throughput is the data actually delivered per unit time end to end; throughput is bounded by the slowest link and is normally lower than the rate.

stem: What is the operation of the following truth table?
explanation: Answer unverified: this item is flagged key_disputed and its truth-table figure is partly degraded. The derived key is B (JK flip flop): the table has two inputs, which rules out T (one input), and every row has a defined output with no forbidden/invalid state, which rules out SR; only JK covers hold, reset, set and toggle in four fully defined rows. The dissent is that if the degraded cells contain no complemented Q, the table would actually describe a gated D flip flop, which is not offered.

stem: which of the following is not a WiFi standard
explanation: IEEE 802.3 is wired Ethernet, so it is the intended odd one out; 802.11 is Wi-Fi itself, and 802.15 (Bluetooth/WPAN) and 802.16 (WiMAX) are at least wireless. Note the question is loosely worded: strictly speaking 802.15 and 802.16 are also not Wi-Fi, but 802.3 is the only non-wireless standard and is the keyed answer.

stem: What is a collision in hash tables?
answerText: When two keys map to the same value
explanation: A collision is when two different keys hash to the same table slot (index). It is unavoidable once the number of keys exceeds the number of buckets (pigeonhole), so hash tables resolve it with chaining (a list per bucket) or open addressing (probe for another slot).
